---
lang: en-US
title: "Parliamentary Expense Radar: Statistical Prioritization with LLM Triage and Human Validation"
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    mainfont: "Times New Roman"
    geometry:
      - top=3cm
      - left=3cm
      - right=2cm
      - bottom=2cm
    fontsize: 12pt
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-block-bg: "#F7F7F7"
    code-block-border-left: false
    highlight-style: github
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 4.5
    fig-pos: H
header-includes:
  - |
    \usepackage{etoolbox}
    \usepackage{graphicx}
    \usepackage{hyperref}
    \usepackage{booktabs}
    \usepackage{threeparttable}
    \usepackage{caption}
    \usepackage{setspace}
    \usepackage{indentfirst}
    \usepackage{array}
    \captionsetup{labelsep=endash,font=small,labelfont=bf}
    \captionsetup[table]{skip=4pt}
    \renewcommand{\maketitle}{}
    \onehalfspacing
    \setlength{\parindent}{1.25cm}
    \AtBeginEnvironment{Shaded}{\footnotesize\singlespacing}
    \AtBeginEnvironment{verbatim}{\footnotesize\singlespacing}
    \AtBeginEnvironment{longtable}{\small\singlespacing}
    \AtBeginEnvironment{tabular}{\small\singlespacing}
    \AtBeginEnvironment{quote}{\footnotesize\singlespacing}
execute:
  eval: true
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{=latex}
% ===== COVER PAGE (mirrors ABNT NBR 14724 layout of the Portuguese paper) =====
\begin{titlepage}
\thispagestyle{empty}
\centering

MBA in AI for Business --- Análise Macro\\
Course: AI Applications

\vspace{2cm}

\includegraphics[width=4cm]{AM.png}

\vspace{1.5cm}

{\huge\bfseries Parliamentary Expense Radar: Statistical Prioritization with LLM Triage and Human Validation\par}

\vspace{1em}

{\Large A reproducible CRISP-DM pipeline to prioritize --- never accuse --- anomalous CEAP expenses\par}

\vspace{2cm}

{\large Alisson Machado\par}

\vfill

{\normalsize São Paulo\par}
{\normalsize July 2026\par}

\end{titlepage}

% ===== TITLE PAGE =====
\begin{titlepage}
\thispagestyle{empty}
\centering

{\large Alisson Machado\par}

\vspace{3cm}

{\huge\bfseries Parliamentary Expense Radar: Statistical Prioritization with LLM Triage and Human Validation\par}

\vspace{1em}

{\Large A reproducible CRISP-DM pipeline to prioritize --- never accuse --- anomalous CEAP expenses\par}

\vspace{2cm}

\begin{flushright}
\begin{minipage}{0.55\textwidth}
\singlespacing
\small
Paper presented to the AI Applications course of the MBA in AI for
Business (Análise Macro), as a partial requirement for course
completion.

\vspace{0.5em}

Professor: Vítor Wilher.
\end{minipage}
\end{flushright}

\vfill

{\normalsize São Paulo\par}
{\normalsize July 2026\par}

\end{titlepage}

% ===== ABSTRACT (primary language of this document) =====
\newpage
\thispagestyle{empty}
\begin{center}
\textbf{ABSTRACT}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\noindent
The Parliamentary Activity Exercise Allowance --- CEAP --- totals 284,845
expense records and R\$345,090,001.79 over 19 months (Jan/2025 to
Jul/2026), far beyond what any single person can review entry by entry.
This work builds a complete CRISP-DM pipeline in three stages. First, it
detects anomalous spending patterns with three independent, robust
statistical methods (median/MAD, supplier-concentration HHI), reducing 601
legislators to 253 flagged by 2+ methods and 26 flagged by all three
simultaneously. Second, it uses an LLM (Claude, via the Anthropic API)
with Pydantic-structured output to classify severity and explain each
case in citizen-facing language, without ever judging anyone's reputation.
Third, it evaluates the LLM across multiple layers: consistency against a
deterministic rubric and agreement against genuine human labeling
(Cohen's kappa = 0.231; 40\% accuracy). The evaluation's central finding
is an LLM collapse bias: 70\% of cases were classified as "medium"
severity and 84\% as "combination" category, almost independently of the
actual signal --- not random noise, but a systematic tendency toward
"safe" answers. The average cost per classified case is US\$0.00525
(model claude-sonnet-4-6), making it cheap to scale classification to
thousands of cases. Every number cited in this abstract is also generated
by an executable code chunk in the body of this document.

\vspace{1em}
\noindent\textbf{Keywords:} Parliamentary Allowance. Anomaly Detection.
Language Models. Structured Output. CRISP-DM.

% ===== RESUMO (Portuguese-language summary, kept for parity with the source paper) =====
\newpage
\thispagestyle{empty}
\begin{center}
\textbf{RESUMO}
\end{center}
\vspace{1em}
\noindent
A Cota para o Exercício da Atividade Parlamentar --- CEAP --- soma 284.845
despesas e R\$ 345.090.001,79 em 19 meses (jan/2025 a jul/2026), longe do
que qualquer pessoa consegue revisar despesa por despesa. Este trabalho
constrói um pipeline CRISP-DM completo em três etapas. Primeiro, detecta
padrões de gasto atípicos com três métodos estatísticos independentes e
robustos (mediana/MAD, HHI de concentração de fornecedor), reduzindo 601
deputados a 253 sinalizados por 2+ métodos e 26 pelos 3 simultaneamente.
Segundo, usa um LLM (Claude, via API Anthropic) com saída estruturada via
Pydantic para classificar severidade e explicar cada caso em linguagem
cidadã, sem nunca julgar reputação de pessoas. Terceiro, avalia o LLM em
múltiplas camadas: consistência contra uma rubrica determinística e
concordância contra rotulagem humana genuína (kappa de Cohen = 0,231;
acurácia 40\%). O achado central da avaliação é um viés de colapso do LLM:
70\% dos casos foram classificados como severidade "média" e 84\% como
categoria "combinação", quase independentemente do sinal real --- não
ruído aleatório, mas uma tendência sistemática a respostas "seguras". O
custo médio de classificação por caso é de US\$ 0,00525 (modelo
claude-sonnet-4-6), o que torna barato escalar a classificação a milhares
de casos. Todo número citado neste resumo também é gerado por um chunk de
código executável no corpo deste documento.

\vspace{1em}
\noindent\textbf{Palavras-chave:} Cota Parlamentar. Detecção de Anomalias.
Modelos de Linguagem. Saída Estruturada. CRISP-DM.

% ===== TABLE OF CONTENTS =====
\newpage
\thispagestyle{empty}
\renewcommand{\contentsname}{TABLE OF CONTENTS}
\tableofcontents

\newpage
\pagenumbering{arabic}
\setcounter{page}{1}
\setlength{\parindent}{1.25cm}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: setup
# Single load of all real pipeline artifacts (data/processed/ and reports/),
# used to cite numbers inline throughout the text. The full code for each
# stage is shown in the Implementation section.
import json
import sys
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

BASE_DIR = Path.cwd().parent.parent
DATA_DIR = BASE_DIR / "data" / "processed"
REPORTS_DIR = BASE_DIR / "reports"
sys.path.insert(0, str(BASE_DIR / "src"))

despesas = pd.read_parquet(DATA_DIR / "despesas_limpas.parquet")
lista = pd.read_csv(REPORTS_DIR / "lista_priorizada.csv", encoding="utf-8-sig")
outliers_cat = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "outliers_categoria_pares.csv", encoding="utf-8-sig")
concentracao = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "concentracao_fornecedor.csv", encoding="utf-8-sig")
picos = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "picos_temporais.csv", encoding="utf-8-sig")
triangulacao = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "triangulacao_deputados.csv", encoding="utf-8-sig")
rubrica = pd.read_csv(REPORTS_DIR / "rotulos_prelabel.csv", encoding="utf-8-sig")
humanos = pd.read_csv(REPORTS_DIR / "rotulos_humanos.csv", encoding="utf-8-sig")
triage = pd.read_csv(REPORTS_DIR / "triage_llm.csv", encoding="utf-8")
custo = json.loads((REPORTS_DIR / "custo_llm_triage.json").read_text(encoding="utf-8"))

n_despesas, n_colunas_raw = despesas.shape
total_gasto = despesas["vlrLiquido"].sum()
n_estornos = int(despesas["flag_estorno"].sum())
n_sem_fornecedor = int((~despesas["fornecedor_identificado"]).sum())
n_deputados_total = len(triangulacao)
n_alta_confianca = int((triangulacao["n_metodos_distintos"] >= 2).sum())
n_tres_metodos = int((triangulacao["n_metodos_distintos"] == 3).sum())
n_lista_priorizada = len(lista)
```

# INTRODUCTION

The Chamber of Deputies publishes CEAP (Parliamentary Activity Exercise
Allowance, *Cota para o Exercício da Atividade Parlamentar*) spending
daily. Within the scope of this project (Jan/2025 to Jul/2026), that
already amounts to `{python} f"{n_despesas:,}"` individual expense records
and R\$`{python} f"{total_gasto:,.2f}"` --- a volume that is humanly
unauditable one entry at a time. Without a way to prioritize, any attempt
at manual oversight is unfeasible: citizens, journalists, and oversight
bodies have no way to review hundreds of thousands of rows to decide where
it is worth looking more closely.

This work's target audience comprises three groups: **citizens** interested
in tracking how their representatives use the allowance; **journalists**
who need concrete starting points for accountability reporting; and
**auditors/oversight bodies** who already have an oversight process in
place but benefit from prior triage that points to where to concentrate
effort.

The goal is to **prioritize, not accuse**. The pipeline does not determine
whether an expense is irregular --- that requires human investigation,
context, and the right to respond. What this work delivers is an objective
statistical ranking of cases that deviate from the expected pattern,
complemented by a classification and explanation layer in language
accessible to the average citizen via an LLM, always with structured,
validated output. The ultimate goal is to allocate the limited attention
of those doing oversight more efficiently.

# LITERATURE REVIEW

The direct precedent for this work, in the same domain (CEAP spending), is
**Operação Serenata de Amor** (MUSSKOPF; CUDUCOS, 2016) (loosely, "Operation
Love Serenade"): an open-source project started in 2016 by Irio Musskopf
and Eduardo Cuducos that uses a machine-learning robot ("Rosie") to compute
a probability of irregularity in Parliamentary Allowance reimbursements and
publish the findings. This work differs in three respects. First, it uses
robust statistical detection (median/MAD) as an auditable layer prior to
any classification, rather than a single probability score. Second, it
uses an LLM only to classify and explain, in terms any reader can
understand, cases already flagged statistically --- never to decide on its
own what is atypical. Third, it includes triangulation across independent
methods and an evaluation of human-LLM agreement (Cohen's kappa
coefficient) as an explicit validation layer, absent from the original
Rosie.

This work's statistical layer follows **Iglewicz and Hoaglin (1993)**:
median and median absolute deviation (MAD) as robust measures of central
tendency and dispersion, and the "modified z-score" (factor 0.6745) as a
practical rule for flagging outliers --- a deliberate choice, since it is
less sensitive to long tails than the classic mean/standard deviation, a
problem well documented in parliamentary spending data itself.

To measure spending concentration in a single supplier, we use the
**Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)** (HIRSCHMAN, 1964), originally a
measure of market concentration in industrial economics, repurposed here
to measure spending concentration per legislator.

To evaluate agreement between the LLM's classification and human labeling,
we use the **Cohen's kappa coefficient** (COHEN, 1960), interpreted using
the reference scale of **Landis and Koch (1977)**.

The LLM classification step follows the official Anthropic API
documentation for output validated by a Pydantic schema (ANTHROPIC, 2026).

# METHODOLOGY AND DATA

## Sources and volume

Data come from the Chamber's public annual files
(`https://www.camara.leg.br/cotas/Ano-{ano}.csv.zip`), years 2025 (complete)
and 2026 (partial, through July). Nulls concentrate in legitimately optional
columns (`txtPassageiro`/`txtTrecho`, populated only for air travel) and in
two peculiarities explicitly handled during data preparation:
`{python} f"{n_estornos:,}"` records with negative `vlrLiquido` (genuine
reversals, kept in the sum) and `{python} f"{n_sem_fornecedor:,}"` records
with no identifiable supplier (generic or missing CNPJ, excluded only from
the supplier-concentration calculation).

## Estimated token cost

The LLM classification step has a measured cost, not one estimated by
analogy: using Anthropic's (free) token-counting API on the prompts
actually sent and the responses received for the
`{python} custo['n_casos']` already-classified cases, the total cost was
US\$`{python} f"{custo['custo_total_usd']:.4f}"`, or **US\$
`{python} f"{custo['custo_medio_por_caso_usd']:.5f}"` per case** (model
`{python} custo['modelo']`, US\$ `{python} custo['preco_input_por_milhao_usd']`
per million input tokens and US\$
`{python} custo['preco_output_por_milhao_usd']` per million output tokens).
The linear projection for the `{python} n_alta_confianca` prioritized cases
(2+ converging methods) is approximately US\$
`{python} f"{custo['projecao_253_casos_usd']:.2f}"` --- making pipeline
extension financially trivial.

## Mapping to CRISP-DM

| CRISP-DM Phase | Deliverable in this project |
|---|---|
| 1. Business Understanding | Problem, target audience, and success criteria (`reports/business_understanding.md`) |
| 2. Data Understanding | Volume, types, nulls, token cost (`reports/data_understanding.md`, above) |
| 3. Data Preparation | Cleaning, typing, flags with no row exclusion (`reports/data_preparation.md`) |
| 4. Modeling | 3 statistical detectors + deterministic baseline + LLM with fixed schema (`reports/modeling.md`) |
| 5. Evaluation | Sensitivity, triangulation, LLM vs. rubric, LLM vs. human (`reports/evaluation.md`, `reports/evaluation_llm.md`) |
| 6. Deployment | Prioritized list, citizen report, this paper (`reports/relatorio_final.md`) |

## LLM structured output

LLM classification uses a fixed Pydantic schema (`TriageAnomalia`), which
forces the fields `severidade` (low/medium/high), `categoria_anomalia`,
`explicacao_cidada` (max. 280 characters, no accusation), and
`perguntas_auditoria` (2 to 3 verifiable audit questions) --- see the full
code in the Implementation section. The LLM only receives cases **already**
statistically flagged; it never decides on its own what is atypical.

## Methodological choices and trade-offs

The decisions below are neither neutral nor inevitable --- they are worth
making explicit, because they shape what the pipeline prioritizes and what
it leaves out.

**Domain**: I chose CEAP over health spending (DataSUS/FNS), another
public-spending domain of comparable volume that I mention in Next Steps
as an extension. I chose CEAP because the Chamber's annual files already
come in a single, stable format, whereas health data would require
integrating multiple sources before any analysis. Given the final
project's deadline, I prioritized delivering a complete pipeline (raw data
→ paper) over deepening the exploration of a domain that is more complex
to integrate.

**Detection method**: I chose median + MAD (IGLEWICZ; HOAGLIN, 1993) over
a machine-learning model (e.g., isolation forest) for the three detectors
in `src/model.py`. In a domain where the result links public figures'
names to an attention ranking, a method whose decision cannot be explained
in one sentence is not acceptable, even if it performed better on some
anomaly-detection metric. Median and MAD are verifiable by hand by anyone
with a calculator.

**Division of labor between statistics and the LLM**: "the LLM never
accuses" is a design decision, not a workaround-able technical limitation.
The LLM does not decide what is atypical: that has already been
established statistically before any prompt. The `src/llm_triage.py`
*system prompt* explicitly forbids any insinuation of wrongdoing, because
the cost of a false positive here is not just any classification error: it
is a person's name attached to an accusation they never committed.

**Error found during exploration**: the first implementation of the
generic-CNPJ filter compared against 12-digit codes instead of 14,
returning zero records when in fact there are 8,527 (see
`reports/data_understanding.md`). The fix only happened because a zero
result was too suspicious to accept without checking.

**Two-pass human labeling**: the first labeling pass over the 50 evaluation
cases used only the category×peers signal, a stricter criterion than the
rubric's definition (`reports/criterio_rotulagem.md`). The second pass
reviewed the 2 cases where this changed the outcome. One of them, Bruno
Ganem, remains a manually decided borderline case (severity "medium", not
"high"): concentration in a Facebook-ads supplier is a common pattern for
parliamentary-outreach campaigns, a judgment call the rubric alone does
not capture.

# IMPLEMENTATION

## Environment setup

This section walks, in code, through the same steps executed by the real
scripts in `src/`: reading credentials, preparing the data, the three
statistical detectors, and the call to the LLM with structured output. The
first step is reading the API key from the environment (`.env`) --- never
written directly into the source code, per the project's secrets rule.

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
# Imports and reading of environment keys (.env). NEVER write the key here.
import os
from pathlib import Path

import pandas as pd
from anthropic import Anthropic
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

load_dotenv(Path(__file__).resolve().parent / ".env")
client = Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment
```

## Data collection and preparation

The real pipeline (`src/explore.py` → `src/prepare.py`) downloads, cleans,
and types the 284,845 raw records without dropping a single row. The chunk
below loads the already processed and verified result
(`despesas_limpas.parquet`) to confirm the counts cited in Methodology:

```{python}
#| echo: true
print(f"Rows: {n_despesas:,} | Columns: {n_colunas_raw}")
print(f"Total spent (vlrLiquido): R$ {total_gasto:,.2f}")
print(f"Reversals (vlrLiquido < 0): {n_estornos:,}")
print(f"No identified supplier: {n_sem_fornecedor:,}")
```

## Statistical modeling (baseline)

Three independent statistical detectors (`src/model.py`), all based on
median + MAD (robust score, threshold 3.5) or HHI (threshold 0.25). They
serve as a **non-LLM baseline** for comparison: a deterministic rubric
(`src/rubric_prelabel.py`) applies the same thresholds with no
subjectivity, and is used in the evaluation as a consistency reference
point for the LLM.

```{python}
#| echo: true
def robust_z(x: pd.Series) -> pd.Series:
    mediana = x.median()
    mad = (x - mediana).abs().median()
    escala = mad if mad > 0 else 1e-6 * (abs(mediana) + 1)
    return 0.6745 * (x - mediana) / escala


print(f"Legislators evaluated (>=1 detector): {n_deputados_total}")
print(f"Flagged by 2+ methods (high confidence): {n_alta_confianca}")
print(f"Flagged by all 3 methods simultaneously: {n_tres_metodos}")
```

## LLM modeling (structured output)

Pydantic schema and the real API call used in production
(`src/llm_triage.py`) --- shown as reference code (`eval: false`, already
executed previously, with its real results loaded in the next chunk so as
not to repeat a paid call). The code below is reproduced verbatim,
including its Portuguese-language docstrings and field descriptions, since
it is the actual production system operating for Brazilian citizens,
journalists, and auditors:

```{python}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
from typing import Literal


class TriageAnomalia(BaseModel):
    severidade: Literal["baixa", "media", "alta"] = Field(
        description="Urgência de verificação humana, dada a magnitude e convergência dos sinais"
    )
    categoria_anomalia: Literal[
        "concentracao_fornecedor", "pico_temporal", "gasto_acima_pares", "combinacao"
    ] = Field(description="Tipo dominante do padrão detectado")
    explicacao_cidada: str = Field(max_length=280, description="Linguagem simples, SEM acusação")
    perguntas_auditoria: list[str] = Field(min_length=2, max_length=3)


MAX_RETRIES = 2  # 1 tentativa + 1 retry com feedback do erro de validação


def classificar(client, caso: str) -> tuple[TriageAnomalia | None, str]:
    """Chama a API e valida com Pydantic; retry com feedback em caso de erro."""
    feedback = ""
    for tentativa in range(1, MAX_RETRIES + 1):
        resposta = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=600,
            system=SYSTEM_PROMPT,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": USER_PROMPT_TEMPLATE.format(caso=caso) + feedback}],
        )
        try:
            dados = json.loads(resposta.content[0].text)
            return TriageAnomalia.model_validate(dados), f"ok_tentativa_{tentativa}"
        except Exception as e:
            feedback = f"\n\nSua resposta anterior falhou na validação: {e}. Corrija."
    return None, "nao_classificado"  # falha final: registro marcado, não descartado
```

Real, already-classified result (`reports/triage_llm.csv`, loaded next ---
no API call is repeated in this render). The category and severity value
counts below are the literal labels recorded by the real pipeline (in
Portuguese), preserved as-is for traceability to the underlying data:

```{python}
#| echo: true
print(triage["status_llm"].value_counts().to_string())
print()
print(triage["severidade"].value_counts().to_string())
```

All `{python} len(triage)` cases were classified successfully on the first
attempt (`ok_tentativa_1`) --- no Pydantic validation retry was necessary.

# RESULTS

## Prioritized list

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
top10 = lista.head(10)

import re

LATEX_SPECIAL = {
    "\\": r"\textbackslash{}",
    "&": r"\&",
    "%": r"\%",
    "$": r"\$",
    "#": r"\#",
    "_": r"\_",
    "{": r"\{",
    "}": r"\}",
    "~": r"\textasciitilde{}",
    "^": r"\textasciicircum{}",
}


def esc(s):
    """Escapes special LaTeX characters (motivo_resumo contains R$ and %)."""
    return re.sub(r"[\\&%$#_{}~^]", lambda m: LATEX_SPECIAL[m.group()], str(s))


print(r"""\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\begin{threeparttable}
\caption{Top 10 cases prioritized by number of converging methods.}
\label{tbl-top10}
\begin{tabular}{p{3.5cm}>{\centering\arraybackslash}p{2cm}p{10cm}}
\toprule
Legislator & Methods (0--3) & Dominant reason \\
\midrule
""")
for _, row in top10.iterrows():
    motivo = esc(str(row["motivo_resumo"])[:70] + "...")
    print(rf"{esc(row['txNomeParlamentar'])} & {row['n_metodos_distintos']} & {motivo} \\")
print(r"""\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\begin{tablenotes}\footnotesize
\item Source: own elaboration from Chamber of Deputies open data (CEAP, 2025-2026).
\item Data via \texttt{reports/lista\_priorizada.csv} (\texttt{src/deploy.py}). Full list with 253 cases in the original file. Names and reason text are reproduced in the original Portuguese, as generated by the real pipeline.
\end{tablenotes}
\end{threeparttable}
\end{table}""")
```

Table `\ref{tbl-top10}`{=latex} shows the highest-confidence cases: all
three statistical methods converging. The full list reduces the universe
from 601 legislators to `{python} n_alta_confianca` flagged by 2 or more
methods --- the central "business finding" of the Modeling/Evaluation
phase.

## Statistical evaluation: threshold sensitivity

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
thresholds_score = [3.0, 3.5, 4.0]
thresholds_hhi = [0.15, 0.25, 0.35]

linhas_cat = [int((outliers_cat["score"].abs() > t).sum()) for t in thresholds_score]
linhas_pico = [int((picos["score"].abs() > t).sum()) for t in thresholds_score]
linhas_hhi = [int((concentracao["hhi"] > t).sum()) for t in thresholds_hhi]

print(r"""\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\begin{threeparttable}
\caption{Sensitivity of flag counts to alternative thresholds (recomputed live).}
\label{tbl-sensibilidade}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
Detector & Low threshold & Default & High threshold \\
\midrule
""")
print(rf"Category×peers (score) & {linhas_cat[0]:,} & {linhas_cat[1]:,} & {linhas_cat[2]:,} \\")
print(rf"Temporal peak (score) & {linhas_pico[0]:,} & {linhas_pico[1]:,} & {linhas_pico[2]:,} \\")
print(rf"Supplier concentration (HHI) & {linhas_hhi[0]:,} & {linhas_hhi[1]:,} & {linhas_hhi[2]:,} \\")
print(r"""\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\begin{tablenotes}\footnotesize
\item Source: own elaboration from Chamber of Deputies open data (CEAP, 2025-2026).
\item Thresholds: score 3.0/3.5/4.0; HHI 0.15/0.25/0.35. Recomputed live from the CSVs in \texttt{data/processed/}.
\end{tablenotes}
\end{threeparttable}
\end{table}""")
```

The count decreases monotonically and gradually across all detectors
(Table `\ref{tbl-sensibilidade}`{=latex}) --- there is no abrupt jump near
the default threshold, indicating that the choice of 3.5/0.25 is not an
artificially sensitive point.

## LLM evaluation: rubric vs. human

```{python}
#| echo: false
from evaluate_llm import cohen_kappa, SEVERIDADES

merged_humano = humanos.merge(triage, on="txNomeParlamentar", how="inner")
confusao_humano = pd.crosstab(merged_humano["severidade_humana"], merged_humano["severidade"])
confusao_humano = confusao_humano.reindex(index=SEVERIDADES, columns=SEVERIDADES, fill_value=0)

accuracy_humano = (merged_humano["severidade_humana"] == merged_humano["severidade"]).mean()
kappa_humano = cohen_kappa(confusao_humano)
concordancia_cat_humano = (merged_humano["categoria_humana"] == merged_humano["categoria_anomalia"]).mean()

merged_rubrica = rubrica.merge(triage, on="txNomeParlamentar", how="inner")
accuracy_rubrica = (merged_rubrica["severidade_prelabel"] == merged_rubrica["severidade"]).mean()

pct_media_llm = (merged_humano["severidade"] == "media").mean()
pct_combinacao_llm = (merged_humano["categoria_anomalia"] == "combinacao").mean()
```

**Central finding of this evaluation: the LLM collapses toward the most
comfortable label instead of reflecting the actual signal.** Compared
against genuine human labeling (n=`{python} len(merged_humano)`), the LLM
achieves only `{python} f"{accuracy_humano:.0%}"` severity accuracy and
**Cohen's kappa = `{python} f"{kappa_humano:.3f}"`** (Table
`\ref{tbl-confusao}`{=latex}) --- on the Landis and Koch (1977) scale,
"fair" agreement, not "substantial". Category agreement is even lower:
`{python} f"{concordancia_cat_humano:.0%}"`.

The cause is not random noise: the LLM classified severity as **"medium"**
in `{python} f"{pct_media_llm:.0%}"` of cases and category as
**"combination"** in `{python} f"{pct_combinacao_llm:.0%}"`, almost
independently of the reference value (human or rubric). Against the
deterministic rubric, accuracy drops to
`{python} f"{accuracy_rubrica:.0%}"` --- even lower, because the rubric
never "hesitates": it is 100% deterministic, and the LLM, without knowing
its thresholds, converges toward the seemingly safe middle ground.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
SEVERITY_LABEL_EN = {"alta": "High", "media": "Medium", "baixa": "Low"}

print(r"""\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\begin{threeparttable}
\caption{Confusion matrix: severity (human) vs. severity (LLM), n=50.}
\label{tbl-confusao}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
Human \textbackslash{} LLM & High & Medium & Low \\
\midrule
""")
for idx, row in confusao_humano.iterrows():
    print(rf"{SEVERITY_LABEL_EN[idx]} & {row['alta']} & {row['media']} & {row['baixa']} \\")
print(r"""\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\begin{tablenotes}\footnotesize
\item Source: own elaboration from Chamber of Deputies open data (CEAP, 2025-2026).
\item Cross-tabulation of \texttt{reports/rotulos\_humanos.csv} and \texttt{reports/triage\_llm.csv} (\texttt{src/evaluate\_llm.py}), recomputed live.
\end{tablenotes}
\end{threeparttable}
\end{table}""")
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-vies
#| fig-cap: "Severity distribution: human label vs. LLM classification (n=50). Source: own elaboration from Chamber of Deputies open data (CEAP, 2025-2026)."
import numpy as np

dist_humano = merged_humano["severidade_humana"].value_counts().reindex(SEVERIDADES, fill_value=0)
dist_llm = merged_humano["severidade"].value_counts().reindex(SEVERIDADES, fill_value=0)

x = np.arange(len(SEVERIDADES))
largura = 0.35

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.bar(x - largura / 2, dist_humano.values, largura, label="Human label", color="#1c2d4f")
ax.bar(x + largura / 2, dist_llm.values, largura, label="LLM classification", color="#18a0d7")
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(["High", "Medium", "Low"])
ax.set_ylabel("Number of cases")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Figure @fig-vies makes the collapse clear: human labeling is distributed
more evenly across the three classes, while the LLM concentrates the
overwhelming majority on "medium" regardless of the case. **Improvement
proposed for a future iteration**: inject the rubric's numeric thresholds
directly into the LLM prompt (R\$50,000 / R\$10,000 / HHI 0.5 / 5+ flags /
partial month), so that classification stops being a blind qualitative
judgment and instead anchors on the same numbers that already guide the
deterministic rubric --- without transferring to the LLM the decision about
what is atypical, only giving it the explicit criteria to decide with more
rigor.

## Cost per case and update plan

The cost of US\$`{python} f"{custo['custo_medio_por_caso_usd']:.5f}"` per
case (measured via `count_tokens`, not estimated by analogy) is negligible
at scale. **Update plan**: the Chamber updates the data daily, but there is
no need to reprocess at that frequency --- a monthly or quarterly cadence
is sufficient. When reprocessing, review whether the thresholds in
`src/model.py` (`SCORE_THRESHOLD = 3.5`, `HHI_THRESHOLD = 0.25`) remain
adequate in light of the sensitivity analysis (Table
`\ref{tbl-sensibilidade}`{=latex}), since flag volume may grow as more 2026
months arrive.

# NEXT STEPS

- **Inject the rubric into the LLM prompt**: give the model explicit
  numeric thresholds (R\$50,000, R\$10,000, HHI 0.5, 5+ flags, partial
  month) to reduce the collapse bias identified in the evaluation, without
  delegating to the LLM the decision about what is atypical.
- **Extension to health spending (DataSUS/FNS)**: the same statistical
  pipeline (median/MAD, HHI, temporal peaks) applies directly to National
  Health Fund transfers by municipality, another auditable, high-volume
  public-spending domain.
- **Monthly report automation**: schedule the full pipeline run
  (`explore → prepare → model → evaluate → deploy → llm_triage`) followed
  by rendering this paper, delivering an updated version with no manual
  intervention.

# CONCLUSION

This work delivered a complete, reproducible CRISP-DM pipeline to
prioritize anomalous Parliamentary Allowance expenses: three robust
statistical detectors reduce `{python} n_deputados_total` legislators to
`{python} n_alta_confianca` priority-attention cases; an LLM with output
validated by a Pydantic schema classifies and explains each one in plain
language, free of technical jargon, at a cost of US\$
`{python} f"{custo['custo_medio_por_caso_usd']:.5f}"` per case; and a
multi-layer evaluation (sensitivity, triangulation, deterministic rubric,
human labeling) shows that the LLM still leans toward the more cautious
judgment (kappa = `{python} f"{kappa_humano:.3f}"`, "fair" rather than
"substantial" on the Landis and Koch (1977) scale) instead of reflecting
each case's actual signal.

The pipeline's statistical part (three detectors + triangulation) is
reproducible and auditable: the reduction to
`{python} n_alta_confianca` priority cases, described above, already
eliminates the need to manually review hundreds of thousands of rows. LLM
classification, on the other hand, should not be used today without human
review of the `explicacao_cidada` it generates: a kappa of
`{python} f"{kappa_humano:.3f}"` is too low to treat the LLM's output as a
verdict. The next iteration described in Next Steps --- injecting the
rubric's thresholds into the prompt --- targets this specific cause; until
it is tested, this work's value lies in the statistical layer, not the
language layer.

# REFERENCES

```{=latex}
\begin{flushleft}
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\singlespacing

ANTHROPIC. \textbf{Anthropic API documentation}. 2026. Available at:
\url{https://docs.anthropic.com}. Accessed on: Jul. 13, 2026.

\bigskip

COHEN, Jacob. A coefficient of agreement for nominal scales.
\textbf{Educational and Psychological Measurement}, v. 20, n. 1, p.
37--46, 1960.

\bigskip

HIRSCHMAN, Albert O. The paternity of an index. \textbf{The American
Economic Review}, v. 54, n. 5, p. 761--762, 1964.

\bigskip

IGLEWICZ, Boris; HOAGLIN, David C. \textbf{How to detect and handle
outliers}. Milwaukee: ASQC Quality Press, 1993.

\bigskip

LANDIS, J. Richard; KOCH, Gary G. The measurement of observer agreement
for categorical data. \textbf{Biometrics}, v. 33, n. 1, p. 159--174, 1977.

\bigskip

MUSSKOPF, Irio; CUDUCOS, Eduardo. \textbf{Operação Serenata de Amor}.
2016. Available at: \url{https://serenata.ai/}. Accessed on: Jul. 13,
2026.

\end{flushleft}
```
